items.question_text: What does a pilot's use of the phrase 'have numbers' indicate regarding ATIS receipt and the controller's obligation to issue landing information?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3a, the controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter when the pilot uses 'have numbers.' However, the note explicitly states that pilot use of 'have numbers' does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. The phrase refers only to having received the required landing data — it is not equivalent to stating an ATIS code. The controller may still need to provide traffic information on a workload-permitting basis.

choices.text: The pilot has received the ATIS broadcast and the controller may omit all landing information.
The pilot has received the required landing data, but this does not indicate ATIS receipt; the controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter.
The pilot is reporting that they have the current METAR and the controller should issue only traffic information.
The controller must still issue full landing information because 'have numbers' is not a recognized phraseology.

items.question_text: Between sunset and sunrise, may a controller use VFR-on-top to separate aircraft holding at the same fix from each other or from en route aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1c, a controller must not clear an aircraft to maintain VFR-on-top between sunset and sunrise to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft unless restrictions are applied to ensure appropriate IFR vertical separation. The concern is that visual see-and-avoid is compromised in darkness, so IFR separation must be maintained.

choices.text: Yes, VFR-on-top may be used to separate holding aircraft at any time provided the aircraft remain in VMC.
Yes, but only if both pilots report the other aircraft in sight and accept visual separation.
No, unless restrictions are applied to ensure appropriate IFR vertical separation.
No, because VFR-on-top is never authorized for holding aircraft regardless of time of day.

items.question_text: When another aircraft is already in the approach sequence ahead of it, under which conditions may a controller still clear a trailing aircraft for a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3c, an aircraft may be cleared for a visual approach when: (1) it is number one in the approach sequence; (2) at a towered airport, it is to follow a preceding aircraft and the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow; or (3) at a towered airport, the pilot reports the airport or runway in sight but not the preceding aircraft — radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided.

choices.text: Only when the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow.
When the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight, OR when the pilot reports the airport/runway in sight with radar separation maintained until visual separation is established.
Whenever the pilot reports the airport in sight, regardless of preceding traffic.
Only when approved separation exists and the aircraft is within 10 miles of the airport.

items.question_text: How does the controller's response differ when handling a pilot-reported flight visibility below 1 mile at an airport with no weather reporting, compared to officially reported ground visibility below 1 mile at an airport with weather reporting?
items.explanation: When weather is not reported and the pilot advises flight visibility below 1 mile, the controller requests the intentions of an arriving aircraft already within the surface area. When ground visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, the controller first informs the aircraft of the reported visibility and then requests intentions using the specific phraseology '(Airport) VISIBILITY LESS THAN 1 MILE. ADVISE INTENTIONS.' The operational distinction is that with no reported weather, the controller relies on pilot-reported flight visibility; with reported weather, the controller cites the official ground visibility measurement.

choices.text: There is no difference; in both cases the controller must deny any clearance.
When no weather is reported, the controller must immediately issue a safety alert; when weather is reported below 1 mile, the controller simply informs the pilot.

When no weather is reported, the controller requests intentions from an aircraft already inside the surface area; when visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, the controller first informs the pilot of the reported visibility and then requests intentions using specific prescribed phraseology.
When no weather is reported, the controller may issue an SVFR clearance; when visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, no clearance is possible.

items.question_text: What must a local controller accomplish before positioning a visually sighted VFR aircraft into the landing sequence?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-3, an aircraft sighted by the local controller at the time of first radio contact may be positioned in the landing sequence only after coordination with approach control. The controller cannot unilaterally insert the aircraft into the sequence — coordination with the radar facility is a prerequisite.

choices.text: Verify that the aircraft has received the current ATIS broadcast.
Coordinate with approach control before positioning the aircraft in the landing sequence.
Confirm the aircraft's transponder is operating and squawking the assigned code.
No additional step is required; the local controller may position any sighted aircraft at their discretion.

items.question_text: Under what two conditions may an approach controller instruct a VFR aircraft to contact the tower before advising the tower of the aircraft's position?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-8b, the aircraft may be instructed to contact the tower prior to the tower being advised of the aircraft's position only when both conditions are met: (1) the tower advises the aircraft is in sight, and (2) space is available in the landing sequence. Both conditions must be satisfied.

choices.text: When the aircraft is within 5 miles of the airport and the tower frequency is not congested.
When both conditions are met: the tower advises the aircraft is in sight AND space is available in the landing sequence.
When the tower has a BRITE or TDW display showing the aircraft's position.
Early transfer is never authorized; the tower must always be advised of position first.

items.question_text: When a control tower is closed, which services is the approach controller authorized to provide to VFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-12, when the tower is not in operation, the controller must provide: (1) wind direction and velocity, (2) traffic information, and (3) notification when radar service is terminated. Assigning a landing sequence is explicitly prohibited per 7-6-12b — the tower normally controls the landing order. All three required services are authorized; only landing sequence assignment is forbidden.

choices.text: Issue wind direction and velocity information, provide traffic information, and inform aircraft when radar service is terminated.
Issue wind direction and velocity information, assign a landing sequence, and provide traffic information.
Assign a landing sequence, issue approach clearances, and provide traffic information.
Terminate all services and instruct aircraft to contact the nearest FSS for advisories.

items.question_text: Does TRSA service replace the basic radar services for VFR aircraft, or is it applied in addition to them?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-1, TRSA procedures are applied IN ADDITION TO the basic services described in Chapter 7, Section 6 (Basic Radar Service to VFR Aircraft - Terminal). TRSA is an overlay — it does not replace basic radar services. The controller must provide both the basic services (safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring, sequencing) and the additional TRSA procedures.

choices.text: Yes, TRSA procedures replace basic radar services entirely within the TRSA boundary.
No, TRSA procedures are applied in addition to the basic radar services described in Chapter 7, Section 6.
TRSA procedures replace basic services only for participating VFR aircraft; non-participating aircraft still receive basic services.
TRSA and basic services are identical; the controller may choose either one.

items.question_text: When a VFR aircraft in a TRSA is assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, when must the controller instruct the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, when a VFR aircraft is assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, the controller must advise the aircraft to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the altitude assignment is no longer needed for separation OR when leaving the TRSA. The prescribed phraseology is 'RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.'

choices.text: Immediately after issuing the altitude assignment, so the pilot knows it is temporary.
When the altitude is no longer needed for separation or when the aircraft leaves the TRSA.
Only when the pilot requests to return to a standard VFR cruising altitude.
Never, because altitude assignments for separation override 14 CFR 91.159 permanently.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose of Class C services?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, Class C services are designed to keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations. The paragraph explicitly states this purpose and notes that operations without radio or transponder can be conducted through LOAs, facility directives, or special arrangements. Class C is about awareness and coordination, not exclusion.

choices.text: To exclude all VFR aircraft from the airspace surrounding primary airports.
To keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations.
To provide full IFR separation services to all aircraft regardless of flight rules.
To ensure that only aircraft with two-way radio communications and transponders may ever operate in the area.

items.question_text: What separation is required between a hot air balloon and an IFR aircraft in Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft in Class C airspace. However, traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate. This is a specific exception to the general Class C separation requirement.

choices.text: Standard Class C separation must be applied; the hot air balloon is a VFR aircraft.
No separation is required; traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.
The balloon must be kept at least 500 feet vertically from all IFR aircraft.
Target resolution separation must be maintained until the balloon exits Class C airspace.

items.question_text: What level of Class C service must aircraft departing satellite controlled airports receive when their route will penetrate Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-7a, aircraft that will penetrate Class C airspace after departing controlled airports within or adjacent to Class C airspace must be provided the same services as those aircraft departing the primary airport. Procedures must be covered in a LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Basic radar services only, since the aircraft is not departing the primary airport.
The same services as aircraft departing the primary airport, with procedures covered in a LOA or facility directive.
No special services; the aircraft must establish contact with approach control after departure.
Only traffic advisories until the aircraft is radar identified inside Class C airspace.

items.question_text: When does Class C service terminate for an aircraft landing at a secondary airport within the outer area?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, unless aircraft are landing at secondary airports or have requested termination of service while in the outer area, services must be provided until the aircraft departs the associated outer area. For an aircraft landing at a secondary airport, service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information. The phraseology is 'CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED' or 'CONTACT (facility identification).'

choices.text: Class C service continues until the aircraft lands at the secondary airport.
Service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the secondary airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate advisory frequency.
Class C service never terminates for aircraft within the outer area, even after landing.
Service terminates automatically when the aircraft leaves Class C airspace proper, regardless of outer area position.

items.question_text: How does the helicopter separation exception in Class B airspace (7-9-6) differ from the helicopter separation exception in Class C airspace (7-8-6)?
items.explanation: In Class B (7-9-6), VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. In Class C (7-8-6a), VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft only. The Class B exception is broader — it exempts separation from both VFR and IFR helicopters/powered-lift, while the Class C exception exempts separation only from IFR helicopters/powered-lift. In both cases, traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.

choices.text: There is no difference; both paragraphs contain identical helicopter separation exceptions.
Class B exempts VFR helicopters from separation from VFR or IFR helicopters/powered-lift; Class C exempts separation only from IFR helicopters/powered-lift.
Class C has no helicopter exception; only Class B provides helicopter separation relief.
Class C exempts all helicopter separation; Class B requires full separation for all helicopters.

lookup_context: Tower-applied visual separation has six conditions under 7-2-1a1; pilot-applied visual separation has different procedures under 7-2-1a2.
items.task: Determine under which subparagraph — tower-applied (7-2-1a1) or pilot-applied (7-2-1a2) — the controller in each scenario should operate, and identify the controlling condition.
items.instruction: For each of the following operational situations, identify whether the controller should apply tower-applied visual separation (7-2-1a1) or pilot-applied visual separation (7-2-1a2), and cite the specific condition that controls.
items.question_text: A terminal controller observes two aircraft on the tower radar display and wants to apply visual separation. The tower visually observes both aircraft. One pilot has been told about the other aircraft. Under which subparagraph does this fall, and what is the controlling requirement?

choices.text: Tower-applied (7-2-1a1): the tower visually observes the aircraft, issues timely traffic advisories, and provides visual separation — this matches (b).
Pilot-applied (7-2-1a2): the pilot must see the other aircraft and be instructed to maintain visual separation.
Neither; visual separation requires both the tower and the pilot to have the aircraft in sight simultaneously.
Tower-applied (7-2-1a1) only if wake turbulence separation is also required.

items.explanation: In this situation, the tower is applying visual separation by visually observing both aircraft and issuing traffic advisories. This is tower-applied visual separation under 7-2-1a1(b). The controller must also maintain communication with at least one aircraft (a) and issue control instructions as necessary (c). Pilot-applied visual separation under 7-2-1a2 requires the pilot to report the other aircraft in sight and accept responsibility for maintaining visual separation.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-3-1b lists five conditions that must be met before an aircraft may be cleared to climb through clouds to VFR-on-top.
items.task: Determine which conditions from 7-3-1b are met in the scenario and whether the controller may issue the clearance.
items.instruction: Review the five conditions in 7-3-1b. For the scenario below, identify which conditions are satisfied and whether the clearance may be issued.
items.question_text: A pilot on an IFR flight plan at 6,000 feet requests a climb through an overcast layer to reach VFR-on-top conditions. The controller has a tops report of 9,000 feet. The controller issues a clearance to climb to 9,000 feet and report reaching VFR-on-top, with an alternative to maintain 9,000 feet and advise if not on top. Upon reaching 9,000 feet, the pilot reports VFR-on-top. Is the controller's handling correct?

choices.text: Yes — all five conditions are met: pilot requested, tops reported, alternative clearance issued, separation ensured, and aircraft recleared VFR-on-top upon reaching.
No — the controller failed to issue the correct phraseology for the alternative clearance.
No — VFR-on-top through a cloud layer requires the pilot to be IFR-rated and current, which was not verified.
Yes — but only the first three conditions apply when tops are reported; conditions 4 and 5 are not required.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b, the five conditions are: (1) pilot requests the clearance, (2) pilot is informed of reported tops height, (3) pilot is informed if no tops report is available, (4) when necessary, separation is ensured by issuing an alternative clearance, and (5) aircraft is recleared VFR-on-top upon reaching. All five conditions are satisfied in this scenario.

items.instruction: Choose the response that correctly describes the controller's obligation.
items.question_text: Compare the controller's responsibility during a go-around from a visual approach at an airport with an operating control tower versus at an airport without an operating control tower. Which statement is correct?

choices.text: At both types of airports, the pilot assumes full responsibility and the controller has no further separation obligations.

At a towered airport, the aircraft may enter the traffic pattern or proceed as instructed, with ATC providing separation. At a non-towered airport, the pilot lands as soon as possible or contacts ATC.
At a towered airport, the pilot must always execute the published missed approach procedure. At a non-towered airport, the pilot must contact ATC for an IFR clearance.
The controller must issue a go-around instruction with a specific altitude in both cases.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a (towered airport), the aircraft may be directed to enter the traffic pattern with no altitude assignment required, or proceed as instructed; ATC must provide approved or visual separation. Per 7-4-1b (non-towered airport), the pilot is expected to complete a landing as soon as possible or contact ATC; ATC must maintain approved separation. A visual approach has no missed approach segment — the go-around is handled as any go-around.

items.instruction: Identify the correct statement about the requirement.
items.question_text: A controller is conducting simultaneous visual approaches to parallel runways separated by 2,000 feet. An aircraft on approach to Runway 27L reports the preceding aircraft on approach to Runway 27R in sight. What must the controller do?

choices.text: Issue the visual approach clearance to Runway 27L without further action, since the pilot has reported the parallel traffic in sight.

Advise the aircraft to maintain visual separation from the preceding aircraft on the adjacent parallel runway, because for runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, sighting the preceding aircraft triggers the visual separation instruction.
Cancel the visual approach to Runway 27L because simultaneous visual approaches are never authorized to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet.
Issue the clearance but vector the aircraft 30 degrees from the extended centerline to reduce overshoot potential.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-4c1, for parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, unless approved separation is provided, an aircraft must report sighting a preceding aircraft making an approach to the adjacent parallel runway. When the pilot reports the other aircraft in sight and visual separation is applied, the controller must advise the succeeding aircraft to maintain visual separation. The 30-degree intercept angle mentioned in the notes relates to reducing overshoot potential but is not the controlling requirement here.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-4-7 lists five conditions (a through e) that must be met before clearing an aircraft for a contact approach.
items.task: Identify which condition from the source text is not met in each scenario, or confirm that all conditions are met.
items.instruction: Review the conditions for a contact approach in 7-4-7a through 7-4-7e. Determine whether the controller may issue the clearance.
items.question_text: An IFR aircraft requests a contact approach to a non-towered airport. The reported ground visibility is 3 miles. The airport has a published and functioning VOR approach. The controller applies approved separation from other IFR traffic by clearing the aircraft at or below 3,000 feet (1,000 feet below the lowest IFR traffic at 4,000 feet) but not below the MSA of 2,200 feet. May the contact approach be issued?

choices.text: Yes — all conditions are met: pilot requested, visibility at least 1 mile, functioning instrument approach published, approved separation applied with correct vertical separation, and no indication weather makes it impracticable.
No — contact approaches may only be issued at airports with operating control towers.
No — the controller initiated the contact approach, which is prohibited; only the pilot may request it. Since the pilot did request it, this is met, but the visibility must be at least 5 miles.
No — the vertical separation minimum for a contact approach is 2,000 feet below any IFR traffic, not 1,000 feet.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-7, all five conditions are met: (a) pilot requested it, (b) ground visibility at least 1 statute mile, (c) published standard or special instrument approach is functioning, (d) approved separation applied — vertical separation uses 'at or below an altitude which is at least 1,000 feet below any IFR traffic but not below the MSA prescribed in 14 CFR 91.119,' and (e) no indication weather makes it impracticable.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-5-1a lists six numbered conditions under which SVFR operations are authorized.
items.task: For each scenario, determine whether all required SVFR authorization conditions are met. Identify any missing condition by number and content.
items.instruction: A pilot requests an SVFR clearance. Using the six conditions in 7-5-1a, determine whether the controller may authorize SVFR.
items.question_text: A pilot of a fixed-wing aircraft requests an SVFR clearance to depart an airport within Class D surface area. The airport weather reports a 600-foot overcast ceiling and 2 miles visibility. The pilot states that VFR cannot be maintained. The airport is not listed in 14 CFR part 91, Appendix D. May the controller issue the SVFR clearance?

choices.text: Yes — all applicable conditions are met: not prohibited by Appendix D, within Class D surface area below 10,000 feet MSL, pilot requested, based on reported weather, and weather is below basic VFR minima.
No — SVFR is never authorized for fixed-wing aircraft.
No — the ceiling must be at least 1,000 feet before SVFR can be authorized.

Yes — but the controller must also apply the provisions of 7-5-1b which prohibits VFR operations beneath a broken or overcast ceiling when the reported ceiling is less than 1,000 feet; an SVFR clearance satisfies this requirement.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, all six conditions are met: (1) the location is not prohibited by 14 CFR part 91 Appendix D, (2) N/A for fixed-wing, (3) within lateral boundaries of Class D surface area below 10,000 feet MSL, (4) pilot requested, (5) based on weather reported at the airport, and (6) N/A — weather IS reported. Per 7-5-1b, VFR operations beneath a broken or overcast ceiling when the reported ceiling is less than 1,000 feet require an SVFR clearance — which is exactly what the pilot is requesting. This condition actually triggers the need for SVFR rather than prohibiting it.

items.instruction: Identify the correct statement about the separation requirement.
items.question_text: Compare the SVFR separation requirements for a fixed-wing aircraft versus a helicopter. Which correctly states the difference?

choices.text: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft may be separated using radar, visual, or nonradar separation, while helicopters may only use visual separation.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft require nonradar or visual separation only; SVFR helicopters may use nonradar, visual, or IFR radar separation.
Both SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and helicopters may use any separation method including radar separation.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft require radar separation only; helicopters may use visual separation only.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-3a, only nonradar or visual separation may be applied between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR aircraft. Radar separation is not authorized due to the cloud clearance and 1-mile flight visibility requirements. Per 7-5-3b, SVFR helicopters may use nonradar, visual, or IFR radar separation. This is a critical operational distinction — controllers must know which separation methods are available based on the aircraft category operating under SVFR.

items.instruction: Choose the statement that correctly identifies the operational difference.
items.question_text: A controller is handling a fixed-wing SVFR aircraft in low visibility at two different airports. At Airport A, the ground visibility is officially reported as 3/4 mile. At Airport B, no weather is reported and the pilot advises that flight visibility is 3/4 mile. Both aircraft are inside their respective surface areas. What is the key operational trigger that distinguishes how the controller handles each aircraft?

choices.text: The distinction is based on aircraft type: helicopters receive different treatment than fixed-wing aircraft.
An official measurement triggers an advisory citing the reported value; a pilot's estimate triggers a request for intentions without an official measurement.
The distinction is based on arrival versus departure: arriving aircraft receive different treatment than departing aircraft.
There is no operational difference; the controller handles both aircraft identically.

items.explanation: When ground visibility is officially reported below 1 mile at the airport, the controller uses the specific phraseology '(Airport) VISIBILITY LESS THAN 1 MILE. ADVISE INTENTIONS' and informs the pilot of the reported value. When weather is not reported and the pilot advises flight visibility is below 1 mile, the controller requests the pilot's intentions without citing an official visibility value. The key distinction is the source and nature of the visibility information — official ground report versus pilot-reported flight visibility.

items.instruction: Select the choice that lists all four required basic radar services for VFR aircraft in the correct order as specified.
items.question_text: What are the four components of basic radar services for VFR aircraft that a terminal controller must provide?

choices.text: Safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring when requested, and sequencing where procedures exist.
Traffic advisories, safety alerts, approach clearances, and landing sequence assignments.
Radar identification, speed adjustments, vectoring for approaches, and traffic advisories.
Sequencing, separation from IFR aircraft, altitude assignments, and traffic advisories.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-1a, basic radar services for VFR aircraft must include: (1) safety alerts, (2) traffic advisories, (3) limited radar vectoring when requested by the pilot, and (4) sequencing at locations where procedures have been established and/or when covered by a LOA. These are the four mandatory components; approach clearances, landing sequence assignments, and IFR separation are not part of basic radar services for VFR aircraft.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-6-7a requires radar contact before instructing a VFR aircraft to enter the traffic pattern or vectoring to the approach sequence. Paragraph 7-6-7b provides for holding when no position is available.
items.task: Identify the required controller action based on the conditions described.
items.instruction: Use the procedures in 7-6-7 to determine the correct controller action.
items.question_text: A VFR aircraft calls approach control for sequencing to the primary airport. The approach sequence for the active runway is full. The controller has radar contact with the aircraft. What should the controller do?

choices.text: Vector the aircraft into the approach sequence anyway, fitting it between two IFR arrivals.
Direct the aircraft to a point near the airport to hold until a position becomes available in the approach sequence.
Instruct the aircraft to contact tower directly and coordinate with approach control for landing sequence.
Terminate radar service and have the aircraft proceed VFR to the airport.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-7b, when a position is not available in the approach sequence for the runway in use, the controller directs the VFR aircraft to a point near the airport to hold. The aircraft may be vectored to another runway after coordination with the tower. This is the appropriate action — not forcing the aircraft into a full sequence or terminating service prematurely.

items.instruction: Choose the statement that correctly describes the controller's obligation.
items.question_text: A controller is holding two VFR aircraft in the TRSA — one inside the TRSA boundary and one outside the TRSA boundary. Does the controller have the same obligation to issue an expected further clearance time for both aircraft?

choices.text: The controller must inform both aircraft when to expect further clearance, because 7-7-2 requires EFC issuance when VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside the TRSA.
The controller must inform only the aircraft inside the TRSA; the aircraft outside the TRSA does not receive TRSA services.
The controller must issue an EFC only if the delay exceeds 10 minutes, regardless of where the aircraft is holding.
EFC issuance is optional in a TRSA; the pilot must request it.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-2, the controller must inform the pilot when to expect further clearance when VFR aircraft are held either inside OR outside the TRSA. The obligation extends to both locations. This is a specific TRSA requirement that differs from some other airspace types where holding procedures may be less prescriptive.

items.instruction: Select the choice that correctly identifies which separation method is available under which condition.
items.question_text: A TRSA controller needs to separate a participating VFR aircraft from an IFR aircraft. The controller is using a display that shows Increased Situation Recognition (ISR). Which separation methods are available and which is affected by ISR?

choices.text: Visual separation and 500 feet vertical separation remain available. Target resolution is available except when ISR is being displayed.
All three methods (visual, vertical, target resolution) are equally available regardless of ISR status.
Only visual separation is available when ISR is being displayed.
Target resolution is only available when ISR is being displayed.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-3, TRSA separation may be achieved by: (a) visual separation, (b) 500 feet vertical separation, or (c) target resolution — except when ISR is being displayed. ISR affects only the third method (target resolution). The other two methods remain fully available.

items.source_label: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Paragraph 7-8-3 and Paragraph 7-9-4
lookup_context: Paragraph 7-8-3 lists three separation methods for Class C. Paragraph 7-9-4 lists separation methods for Class B with two weight-based tiers.
items.task: Compare the separation methods available in Class C versus Class B airspace and identify the key operational difference.
items.instruction: Review the separation methods in 7-8-3 (Class C) and 7-9-4 (Class B). Identify the key structural difference.
items.question_text: A controller is separating VFR aircraft from IFR aircraft in Class C airspace. How does the separation framework differ from Class B airspace?

choices.text: Class C uses uniform separation methods for all aircraft. Class B separates methods by weight: 1.5 miles for heavier aircraft, target resolution for lighter ones.
Class C and Class B use identical separation frameworks; there is no difference.
Class C requires 3 miles separation between all VFR and IFR aircraft; Class B requires 5 miles.
Class C has no visual separation option; Class B permits visual separation.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-3, Class C separation applies uniformly to all VFR aircraft being separated from IFR: visual, 500 feet vertical, or target resolution (except when ISR displayed). Per 7-9-4, Class B has a two-tier weight-based system: VFR aircraft separated from aircraft over 19,000 lbs/turbojets must use 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; VFR aircraft separated from aircraft 19,000 lbs or less use target resolution (except ISR), 500 feet vertical, or visual. The Class B framework is more complex with weight as the differentiator.

items.instruction: Identify the correct separation requirement for each scenario.
items.question_text: A Class B controller needs to separate VFR aircraft from two different IFR aircraft: an Airbus A320 (turbojet, over 19,000 lbs) and a Cessna 172 (under 19,000 lbs). Which separation methods are available for each?

choices.text: From the A320: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual. From the Cessna 172: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual.
From both: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; target resolution is available for both.
From the A320: 3 miles only. From the Cessna 172: 1.5 miles only.
From both: 500 feet vertical or visual only; radar miles separation is not available in Class B.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-4b, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 lbs and turbojets by: 1.5 miles (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. Per 7-9-4c, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing 19,000 lbs or less by: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. The weight threshold creates two distinct separation tiers.

items.front: What does a pilot's use of the phrase 'have numbers' indicate to approach control?
items.back: The pilot has received the required landing data (wind, runway, altimeter). It does NOT indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. The controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter information but must still provide traffic information on a workload-permitting basis.

items.front: Under what two conditions is visual separation NOT authorized regardless of other factors?
items.back: 1. When the lead aircraft is a super. 2. When wake turbulence separation is required (for tower-applied visual separation). Additionally, tower-applied visual separation is not authorized between successive departures when departure routes and/or aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation.

items.front: What phraseology does a controller use to inform an aircraft of anticipated delay when an SVFR clearance cannot be granted because of IFR traffic?
items.back: EXPECT (number) MINUTES DELAY, (additional instructions as necessary). Do not issue an EFC or expected departure time.

items.front: In TRSA operations, what separation is required between two VFR helicopters?
items.back: Helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic information must be exchanged as necessary. This differs from Class B and Class C helicopter exceptions which specify separation relief only from IFR helicopters (Class C) or from VFR/IFR helicopters (Class B).

items.front: What four services does Class C service include?
items.back: 1. Sequencing of all aircraft to the primary airport. 2. Standard IFR services to IFR aircraft. 3. Separation, traffic advisories, and safety alerts between IFR and VFR aircraft. 4. Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.

items.front: When must a Class C controller instruct a VFR aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.back: When an assigned altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when terminating Class C service. Phraseology: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

items.front: In Class B airspace, when must a controller instruct a VFR aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes after assigning an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159?
items.back: When the altitude assignment is no longer required or when leaving Class B airspace. Phraseology: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.
